texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Range Description for an Attenuated Conical Radon Transform with Fixed Central Axis and Opening Angle
texts.text: Range Description for an Attenuated Conical Radon Transform with Fixed Central Axis and Opening Angle

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gihyeon Jeon *
texts.text: Gihyeon Jeon *

texts.label: text
texts.orig: School of Mathematics, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea
texts.text: School of Mathematics, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

texts.label: text
texts.orig: * Corresponding author: rydbr6709@knu.ac.kr
texts.text: * Corresponding author: rydbr6709@knu.ac.kr

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The conical Radon transform is an integral transform that maps a given function f to its integral over a conical surface. In this study, we invesgate the conical Radon transform with a fixed central axis and opening angle, considering the attenuation of radiation within the transform. Specifically, we explore the attenuated conical Radon transform. In this paper, we provide the range conditions for the attenuated conical Radon transform and its auxiliary transform. Range description of an operator is an important topic in mathematics, and it is useful for understanding the transform, completing incomplete data, improving reconstuction algorithm, correcting measurement error. The range conditions of attenuated conical Radon transforms are given in terms of the hyperbolic differential operator.
texts.text: The conical Radon transform is an integral transform that maps a given function f to its integral over a conical surface. In this study, we invesgate the conical Radon transform with a fixed central axis and opening angle, considering the attenuation of radiation within the transform. Specifically, we explore the attenuated conical Radon transform. In this paper, we provide the range conditions for the attenuated conical Radon transform and its auxiliary transform. Range description of an operator is an important topic in mathematics, and it is useful for understanding the transform, completing incomplete data, improving reconstuction algorithm, correcting measurement error. The range conditions of attenuated conical Radon transforms are given in terms of the hyperbolic differential operator.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Radon transform, Cone transform, Range description, Compton camera
texts.text: Keywords: Radon transform, Cone transform, Range description, Compton camera

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The conical Radon transform is an integral transform that assigns to a function f on R 3 its surface integral over a cone. Interest in this transform has increased significantly since the introduction of the Compton camera [1]. The Compton camera was initially devised for single-photon emission computed tomography. However, it has been applied in various fields, including astronomy, singlescattering optical tomography, and homeland security [2].
texts.text: The conical Radon transform is an integral transform that assigns to a function f on R 3 its surface integral over a cone. Interest in this transform has increased significantly since the introduction of the Compton camera [1]. The Compton camera was initially devised for single-photon emission computed tomography. However, it has been applied in various fields, including astronomy, singlescattering optical tomography, and homeland security [2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Studies on the Compton cameras and various conical Radon transforms are presented in [3-17]. However, most of these studies do not consider radiation attenuation, a crucial factor in image reconstruction. Neglecting the attenuation effect leads to inaccurate image reconstruction [18]. We thus consider the attenuated conical Radon transform, which is defined as follows: For µ > 0 and fixed angle ψ ∈ (0 , π/ 2), the attenuated conical Radon transform C µ f of f ∈ C ∞ c ( R n × R ) is defined by
texts.text: Studies on the Compton cameras and various conical Radon transforms are presented in [3-17]. However, most of these studies do not consider radiation attenuation, a crucial factor in image reconstruction. Neglecting the attenuation effect leads to inaccurate image reconstruction [18]. We thus consider the attenuated conical Radon transform, which is defined as follows: For µ > 0 and fixed angle ψ ∈ (0 , π/ 2), the attenuated conical Radon transform C µ f of f ∈ C ∞ c ( R n × R ) is defined by

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: C µ f ( u , v ) = ∞ ∫ 0 ∫ S n -1 f (( u , v ) + ( r sin ψ ω , r cos ψ )) e -µr ( r sin ψ ) n -1 d S ( ω )d r = tan n -1 ψ cos ψ ∫ S n -1 ∞ ∫ 0 f (( u , v ) + ( z tan ψ ω , z )) e -µ cos ψ z z n -1 d S ( ω )d z,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where ( u , v ) = ( u 1 , . . . , u n , v ) ∈ R n × R and the variable is changed by r cos ψ ≡ z .
texts.text: where ( u , v ) = ( u 1 , . . . , u n , v ) ∈ R n × R and the variable is changed by r cos ψ ≡ z .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More research is needed on the attenuated conical Radon transform. M. Halrmeire et al. proposed the attenuated V-line transform, a two-dimensional variant of the attenuated conical Radon transform, and a reconstruction algorithm using circular detectors [18]. R. Gouia-Zarrad and S. Moon investigated the inversion formula for the n -dimensional attenuated conical Radon transform with a fixed opening angle and provided numerical results [19]. G. Jeon and S. Moon studied the inversion formula of the attenuated conical Radon transform with a fixed opening angle constucted using singular value decomposition [20]. The generalization of the attenuated conical Radon transform has been further investigated in [21, 22]. This study describes the range of the attenuated conical Radon transform and its auxiliary transform. The range conditions are expressed in terms of the hyperbolic differential operator.
texts.text: More research is needed on the attenuated conical Radon transform. M. Halrmeire et al. proposed the attenuated V-line transform, a two-dimensional variant of the attenuated conical Radon transform, and a reconstruction algorithm using circular detectors [18]. R. Gouia-Zarrad and S. Moon investigated the inversion formula for the n -dimensional attenuated conical Radon transform with a fixed opening angle and provided numerical results [19]. G. Jeon and S. Moon studied the inversion formula of the attenuated conical Radon transform with a fixed opening angle constucted using singular value decomposition [20]. The generalization of the attenuated conical Radon transform has been further investigated in [21, 22]. This study describes the range of the attenuated conical Radon transform and its auxiliary transform. The range conditions are expressed in terms of the hyperbolic differential operator.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The range of tomographic transforms, such as the Radon transform, is essential in tomographic imaging. Understanding the range of these transforms is crucial, as it provides significant insight into the measurement data and the underlying functions being reconstructed. Information about the range of tomographic transforms is useful for several reasons: it aids in comprehending the transforms themselves, completing incomplete data, improving reconstruction algorithms, and correcting measurement errors [23,24]. There have been various studies on the range of different transforms. G. Ambartsoumian studied the range of the two-dimensional circular Radon transform [25]. M. Agranovsky et al. provided range conditions for the spherical mean Radon transform [23]. A. Katsevich and R. Krylov presented inversion formulas and range conditions for the broken ray transform [26]. W. Baines studied the range of conical Radon transform [17], and we apply his ideas to the attenuated conical Radon transform.
texts.text: The range of tomographic transforms, such as the Radon transform, is essential in tomographic imaging. Understanding the range of these transforms is crucial, as it provides significant insight into the measurement data and the underlying functions being reconstructed. Information about the range of tomographic transforms is useful for several reasons: it aids in comprehending the transforms themselves, completing incomplete data, improving reconstruction algorithms, and correcting measurement errors [23,24]. There have been various studies on the range of different transforms. G. Ambartsoumian studied the range of the two-dimensional circular Radon transform [25]. M. Agranovsky et al. provided range conditions for the spherical mean Radon transform [23]. A. Katsevich and R. Krylov presented inversion formulas and range conditions for the broken ray transform [26]. W. Baines studied the range of conical Radon transform [17], and we apply his ideas to the attenuated conical Radon transform.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The rest of this paper is structured as follows. In the next subsection, we present definitions, notations, and lemmas. Section 2 outlines the main results regarding the range conditions for the operators C µ . Detailed proofs of these results are provided in Section 3.
texts.text: The rest of this paper is structured as follows. In the next subsection, we present definitions, notations, and lemmas. Section 2 outlines the main results regarding the range conditions for the operators C µ . Detailed proofs of these results are provided in Section 3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Main Results
texts.text: 2 Main Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We now present the main results of this study. A necessary and sufficient condition for g ∈ C ∞ ( R n +1 ) is that L k g ( k ∈ N ) has compact support. The range conditions for the operators C µ are given in the following Theorems.
texts.text: We now present the main results of this study. A necessary and sufficient condition for g ∈ C ∞ ( R n +1 ) is that L k g ( k ∈ N ) has compact support. The range conditions for the operators C µ are given in the following Theorems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1. Let n = 2 k -1 be odd (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k -1 ). A function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k -1 × R ) is in the range of C µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k -1 × R ) if and only if the following conditions are satisfied:
texts.text: Theorem 1. Let n = 2 k -1 be odd (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k -1 ). A function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k -1 × R ) is in the range of C µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k -1 × R ) if and only if the following conditions are satisfied:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (i) L k g ( x, z ) has compact support.
texts.text: (i) L k g ( x, z ) has compact support.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (ii) ∞ ∫ -∞ e -µ cos ψ z L k g ( x, z )d z = 0 for every x ∈ R 2 k -1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the specific case where n is even, the operator A µ is used to demonstrate the range conditions of C µ .
texts.text: In the specific case where n is even, the operator A µ is used to demonstrate the range conditions of C µ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 2. Let n = 2 k be even (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k ). A function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k × R ) is in the range of C µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k × R ) if and only if the following conditions are satisfied:
texts.text: Theorem 2. Let n = 2 k be even (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k ). A function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k × R ) is in the range of C µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k × R ) if and only if the following conditions are satisfied:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (i) L 2 k A µ g ( x, z ) has compact support.
texts.text: (i) L 2 k A µ g ( x, z ) has compact support.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: (ii) ∞ ∫ -∞ e -µ cos ψ z L 2 k A µ g ( x, z )d z = 0 for every x ∈ R 2 k

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorems regarding range conditions for A µ are analogous to those for C µ but are more straightforward, as shown in the following.
texts.text: Theorems regarding range conditions for A µ are analogous to those for C µ but are more straightforward, as shown in the following.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 3. Let n = 2 k -1 be odd (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k -1 ). For a function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k -1 × R ) , g is in the range of A µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k -1 × R ) if and only if L k -1 g has compact support.
texts.text: Theorem 3. Let n = 2 k -1 be odd (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k -1 ). For a function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k -1 × R ) , g is in the range of A µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k -1 × R ) if and only if L k -1 g has compact support.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the specific case where n is odd, the range conditions of A µ are related to L 2 k -1 A µ g , similar to Theorem 2.
texts.text: In the specific case where n is odd, the range conditions of A µ are related to L 2 k -1 A µ g , similar to Theorem 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 4. Let n = 2 k be odd (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k ). For a function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k × R ) , g is in the range of A µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k × R ) if and only if L 2 k -1 A µ g has compact support.
texts.text: Theorem 4. Let n = 2 k be odd (i.e., x ∈ R 2 k ). For a function g ∈ C ∞ ( R 2 k × R ) , g is in the range of A µ on C ∞ c ( R 2 k × R ) if and only if L 2 k -1 A µ g has compact support.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4 Conclusion
texts.text: 4 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we studied the range conditions for the operators C µ and A µ . We demonstrated that differential operator L is vital in describing the range conditions for C µ and A µ . Additionally, we showed that the range conditions for C µ and A µ vary depending on the dimension of the domain. Our results contribute significantly to the understanding of the attenuated conical Radon transforms. Furthermore, in a future study, we plan to address the inversion and range description of generalized attenuated conical Radon transforms.
texts.text: In this paper, we studied the range conditions for the operators C µ and A µ . We demonstrated that differential operator L is vital in describing the range conditions for C µ and A µ . Additionally, we showed that the range conditions for C µ and A µ vary depending on the dimension of the domain. Our results contribute significantly to the understanding of the attenuated conical Radon transforms. Furthermore, in a future study, we plan to address the inversion and range description of generalized attenuated conical Radon transforms.
